coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot-assisted VO2(D) welding on holey graphene for zinc-ion battery cathode materials
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Luchen
Li, Xinyu
Lu, Guilin
Wang, Yiwen
Zhang, Zhen
coredata.dc:description: Superior-quality zinc-ion batteries utilizing nanopseudocapacitor materials and flexible, three-dimensional, highly conductive porous carbon materials are current research hotspots. But the inadequate dispersion between the system components and the dissolution of the pseudocapacitive materials severely limit the further application of VO2-watery zinc-ion battery components (ZIBs). In this work, we create a novel type of independent VO2(D) covered with carbon quantum dots and assist in its welding on holey graphene (CVH). Composite structures of graphene-supported VO2(D) were designed by a simple solvothermal reaction and ultrasonic self-assembly. The CQD coating acts as a conductive medium, facilitating charge transfer to the surface and also mechanically stabilizing the cathode during volume changes, while the CQD acts as a “bridge” to form an intimate interfacial relationship with the HG. The introduction of HG not only provides more sites of activity and longitudinal transport pathways for electron and ion diffusion, but also makes the overall structure more stable and effective in eliminating the effect of volume change and realizing large pseudocapacitance. The electrochemical results show that CVH has an ultra-strong specific capacity of 443 mA h g−1 at 0.1 A/g, and a capacity retention of 332 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles at 0.5 A/g. Excellent electrochemical performance for Zn2+ ion storage is found in CVH. This unusual structure may help us understand developing ZIB cathodes better while also offering fresh perspectives on high-performance batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: VO2(D)
Carbon Quantum Dot Coating
Holey graphene
Aqueous Zn-ion batteries

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dot solution
Performance of aqueous zinc-ion batteries electrochemical and kinetic

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices
Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region of China ( 2018GXNSFAA281191 ), Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices (20KF-20).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The composition and architecture of the CVH composite.

Characterizations of structure and morphology.(a) Typical tem images of rGO, (b) The etched holes on 3DHG. (c) SEM of VO2(D). (d) CVH morphology and structure of SEM. (e) TEM images of CVH. (f) TEM image of the quantum dot coating. (g) EDS image of the CVH.

(a) XRD patterns for VO2(D) and CVH (b) TGA curves of CVH and VO2(D). (c) Raman spectra of CVH and VO2(D). (d) The isotherm for N2 adsorption/desorption and the corresponding pore size distribution of VO2(D) and CVH.
XPS spectra of CVH: (a) Survey spectra of CVH composite, (b) C 1 s, (c) V 2p, and (d)O 1 s.

Electrochemical performance of CVH and VO2(D). (a) CV curves at a rate of 0.1 mV/s. (b) CV at 0.1 mV s−1 for both VO2(D) and CVH. (c) Curves of discharge and charge at 0.1 A/g. (d) Rate performance. (e) Electrode charge–discharge curves at varying current rates. (f) Three hundred and fifty cycling stability at 3 A/g.(g) Stability of long-term cycling at 0.5 A/g.

Electrochemical kinetic characterization of CVH electrodes. (a) CV curves at various scan frequencies. (b) Plots of the redox peaks that correspond to the CV in terms of log (i) and log (v). (c) Contribution of capacitance capacity at different scan rates (d) Capacitance separation curve at 0.5 mV s−1 (e) Capacitance separation curve at 1 mV s-1data. (f) The fitted analogous circuit models are represented by the EIS spectra (insert image).(g) GITT cures of CVH and VO2(D). The ion diffusion coefficients of VO2,CVH during the charge/discharge.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot-assisted VO2(D) welding on holey graphene for zinc-ion battery cathode materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Superior-quality zinc-ion batteries utilizing nanopseudocapacitor materials and flexible, three-dimensional, highly conductive porous carbon materials are current research hotspots. But the inadequate dispersion between the system components and the dissolution of the pseudocapacitive materials severely limit the further application of VO2-watery zinc-ion battery components (ZIBs). In this work, we create a novel type of independent VO2(D) covered with carbon quantum dots and assist in its welding on holey graphene (CVH). Composite structures of graphene-supported VO2(D) were designed by a simple solvothermal reaction and ultrasonic self-assembly. The CQD coating acts as a conductive medium, facilitating charge transfer to the surface and also mechanically stabilizing the cathode during volume changes, while the CQD acts as a “bridge” to form an intimate interfacial relationship with the HG. The introduction of HG not only provides more sites of activity and longitudinal transport pathways for electron and ion diffusion, but also makes the overall structure more stable and effective in eliminating the effect of volume change and realizing large pseudocapacitance. The electrochemical results show that CVH has an ultra-strong specific capacity of 443 mA h g−1 at 0.1 A/g, and a capacity retention of 332 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles at 0.5 A/g. Excellent electrochemical performance for Zn2+ ion storage is found in CVH. This unusual structure may help us understand developing ZIB cathodes better while also offering fresh perspectives on high-performance batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last few years, aqueous zinc ion batteries (ZIBs), have drew multiple interest because of their high theoretical capacity (819 mAh/g), low redox potential (-0.76 V compared to standard hydrogen electrodes), low cost, and high security [1–4]. As AZIB cathodes, layered vanadium-based oxides are currently very advanced [5–8]. Layered vanadium oxide (VO2) has open tunnel-like frameworks and a higher electrode potential than other vanadium oxides. VO2 is a prospective cathode stuff that has the potential to provide a high theoretical capacity of 646 mAh/g through a two-electron redox reaction [7,9–12]. But because Zn2+ has a significant atomic mass/charge radius and strong positive polarity, it frequently exhibits slow kinetics, irreversible phase formation, and structural degradation upon insertion or extraction from the crystal structure [13–15]. Recently, composite structures consisting of porous graphene (HG) and pseudocapacitive materials combining the characteristics of pseudocapacitance and porous structures have received increasing attention in solving these problems [16,17]. HG has abundant in-plane nanopores to facilitate ion transport along the longitudinal direction and realize rate performance. Furthermore, HG nanosheets can be employed as a conducting framework to hasten electron transmission and preserve the electrode material's structural integrity. However, this strategy is rarely reported [17–20].

VO2(D) with high surface activity has poor monodispersity in composites, and simple composites will make a large region in touch with the electrolyte while increasing the generation of side reactions and causing the dissolution of the electrode materials, resulting in rapid capacity degradation and low utilization of active material [21,22]. A crucial but frequently ignored fact is that VO2(D)'s low physical confinement and poor interface compatibility with HG cause stress-driven VO2(D) extrusion and free movement during conversion, as well as additional agglomeration and irreversible separation from the substrate, decreased cycling stability, and even battery failure. These issues present difficult kinetic problems [21,23,24]. To this end, special attention was paid to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as flexible linkers to form the specific bonding environments and interfacial interactions between HG and VO2(D) because of their hyperdispersive properties, crystalline sp2-hybridized core, and abundance of edge sites [25–29]. In addition, the combination of carbon quantum dots with excellent hydrophilicity and VO2(D) helps to improve the interfacial stability between VO2 and the electrolyte and reduce the interfacial resistance, which contributes to the improvement of the cycle life and stability of the battery [30,31].

Herein, we report on the application of CQD coating to modify the surface of VO2 (D) and assist its welding on the surface of HG (CVH), and the HG in this structure provides a flexible substrate. The distance that the electrolyte ions must travel to move through the electrode material is shortened by the longitudinal channels for ion diffusion that the etched holes on the HG also provide. On the other hand, the CQD coating not only acts as a protective agent and sensitizer, but the interface formed between it and the pseudo-capacitive material has a special electronic structure and energy band alignment, which can promote electron transport and carrier migration. In addition, the CQD-assisted VO2 (D) welded to the HG ensures the stability and integrity of the structure. As expected, the CVH electrode reaches a very impressive 443 mAh/g capacity at 0.1 A/g, and compared to the pristine VO2 with minimal capacity output, the CVH can have a capacity retention of 332 mAh/g after 1,000 cycles at 0.5 A/g and a high reversible capacity of 240 mAh/g at 3 A/g.This study will provide more effective ways to study more ZIB electrode materials.

Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co. was the source of the commercial V2O5, oxalic acid, methanol, and sucrose, which were used directly. We directly used H2O2, anhydrous ethanol, and natural graphite that we bought from China Pharmaceutical and Chemical Reagent Company. GO nanosheet synthesis employs the Hummer technique [32]. In this experiment, all aqueous solutions refer to deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dot solution
ce:section.ce:para: CQD solutions were prepared based on previous research results [25]. 1.71 g of sucrose were usually dissolved in 50 ml of water and added to a 50 ml Teflon reactor for the synthesis. After three hours of heating at 160 °C, the reactor was allowed to cool to room temperature. Following cooling, the mixture was centrifuged, and the resulting precipitate was gathered and dried for a full day at 80 °C in an oven. After that, the powder was sonicated and dissolved in ethanol. After centrifuging sonicated solution, the precipitated solution was utilized as a CQD solution to prepare CVH samples.

Graphene oxide nanosheets were synthesized using the Hummers method. 7 mg of graphene oxide was taken and mixed with 3.5 ml of deionized water and 0.5 ml of a 30 % H2O2 solution. After the solution was mixed, it was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a 10 ml reactor and hydrothermally heated at 90 ℃ for 3.5 h. The obtained hydrogel (HG) was washed with deionized water to remove any residual hydrogen peroxide solution and then freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain the final HG.

100 ml of deionized water was combined with 3.56 g of oxalic acid and commercially available V2O5 powder. The mixture was shaken at 60 °C for a while until the solution turned blue. It was then dried at 75 °C for two days and ground for additional use. After that, 50 ml of a methanol/water (4:1, v/v) mixture was mixed with more than 100 mg of solid vanadium oxalate. After 30 min of ultrasonication, the suspension was moved to a 100-ml PTFE-sealed autoclave and hydrothermally reacted for 24 h at 200 °C. After that, the VO2 (D) was repeatedly cleaned with water and alcohol. After being submerged in CQD solution, a specific volume of VO2 (D) was left to stand at room temperature for six hours. Following that, HG was added in a specific amount and sonicated for 30 min. After that, freeze-drying was done, and lastly, annealing took three hours at a temperature of 300 °C in an argon atmosphere while heating at a rate of five degrees Celsius per minute. After cooling, CVH hybrids were created.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G220 TWIN, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, TESCAN MIRA LMS, Czech Republic) were used to assess the morphology of the materials. The SEM was utilized to analyze the elements in the composites and look into their elemental composition using an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS S-00123, USA). Using an automated SSA and porosity analyzer (ASAP 2460), N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured at −196 °C. The adsorption techniques of Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) were utilized to ascertain the mesopore size distribution and SSA of every sample. The crystalline phase and composition of each sample were ascertained by means of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution, Japan) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, MiniFlex-600, Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). A Horiba Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer was used to measure the Raman spectra at 512 nm. Thermogravimetric analysis equipment (SDT Q600, USA) was used to test TGA/DTA, heating it up to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C per minute from room temperature.

Zn was used as anode, glass fiber membrane was used as diaphragm, and 3 M Zn(CF3SO3)2 was used as electrolyte in a CR2016 button cell for studying the Zn2+ storage properties of CVH. The anode was prepared by mixing the synthesized active substance, conductive agent (Super-P), and pvdf in a mass ratio of 7:2:1. The resulting well-milled slurry was uniformly loaded on a stainless steel mesh and subsequently dried under vacuum at 65 ℃ for 12 h. The active substance was loaded on the electrode at about 2 mg/cm−2. Between 0.2 and 1.4 V (vs. Zn/Zn2+), galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements and galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) experiments were conducted continuously. on a Neware battery tester (CT 4008). An electrochemical workstation (CHI-660D) was used for the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments. Impedance measurements were made (zinc extraction) after the batteries were fully charged.

The schematic of the synthesis of CVH is shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, thin graphene sheets were etched by mixing GO nanosheets with H2O2. Hydrogen peroxide is used to etch defective sites on the graphene oxide (GO) substrate. This process produces carbon vacancies, which eventually spread into irregular and dispersed holes (designated as HG). This not only provided abundant mesopores for ion longitudinal transport and electron transfer pathways, which contributed to the penetration between the electrode and electrolyte interfacial regions, but also provided sufficient void space for soldering of VO2(D). The prepared VO2(D) is then immersed in a CQD solution, and the GQDs are negatively charged due to the carboxyl groups on their surfaces. During the contact process, they can be tightly fixed to the positively charged surface of VO2 (D). The CQD is made like a film coated on the surface of VO2, and finally the CQD-coated VO2 and HG are mixed, followed by low-power ultrasonication, and the CQD-coated VO2 nanorods are uniformly adsorbed on the surface of HG through the interaction force. Meanwhile, CQD is extremely hydrophilic, which helps to reduce the polarization problem during the charging and discharging process of zinc-ion batteries, and at the same time, it can effectively adsorb the zinc ions and promote their reaction, so as to reduce the performance degradation of the batteries during cycling. Finally, the resulting material was freeze-dried to a black powder and then calcined under and argon atmosphere to reduce HG to obtain the sample (CVH).

We utilized SEM and TEM to characterize the microstructure of the sample material. From Fig. 2 a, we can see that graphene oxide without etching has a smooth surface. After H2O2 etching, we can observe more regular and uniformly distributed holes on the graphene substrate, as in Fig. 2b, indicating that the GO nanosheets were successfully etched. The design of this porous structure not only reduces the transport path of Zn2+, but additionally offers more active sites to boost the capacity for adsorption and a embedding. The figure c shows that the prepared VO2 samples exhibit good spherical structure and highly uniform size. From figure d, it can be seen that the nanospheres with a diameter of about 500 nm are uniformly dispersed on the HG surface. The figure e shows the TEM images of the prepared CVHs. The figure shows the lattice stripe spacing of 0.229 nm, 0.246 nm, 0.34 nm, and 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (200) and (002) faces of VO2 (D) as well as the (200) and (002) faces of HG and (002) as well as the (101) faces of CQD. The produced CQDs, as seen in Figure. S1, S2, have a uniform size distribution with a size range of roughly 2–10 nm. The prepared CQDs' TEM lattice diagrams are shown in the insets. Additionally, it is demonstrated that the lattice spacing is approximately 0.20 nm, in agreement with other references [25,33]. and Fig. 2f shows that the prepared CQD coating is uniformly dispersed on the VO2(D) surface, which has a thickness of about 4–5 nm and shows a lattice spacing of about 0.20 nm, and it can be seen that the CQD and the VO2 materials are well composited together and form a good interface. CQDs are mainly distributed on the surface of the composite material as a coating encapsulated on the surface of VO2(D) nanospheres. It not only plays the role of sensitization and protection, but the interface formed between the carbon quantum dots and the pseudocapacitive material has a special electronic structure and energy band alignment, which can promote electron transport and carrier migration. In addition, the combination of carbon quantum dots with excellent hydrophilicity and VO2(D) helps to improve the interfacial stability between VO2 and the electrolyte and reduce the interfacial resistance, which contributes to the improvement of the cycle life and stability of the battery (Figure S4). The interfacial effect can increase the migration rate of electrons in the composites and thus improve their electrical conductivity. The EDS of CVH (Fig. 2f) From the figure, it can be seen that C,O andV, are uniformly dispersed in the material, and it is worth noting that the uniform distribution of carbon indicates that the composites have a strong electrical conductive network with a tight interfacial connection.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was used to look into the two samples' structural data. When compared to the XRD map of pristine VO2(D) (JCPDS NO.15–0755), several of the major peaks of CVH show no discernible changes. It is noteworthy that there are several small peaks around 25° attributed to the (001) and (101) of V2O5 (JCPDS 41–1426), which may be due to a small amount of the VO2 surface being oxidized or the graphene broad (002) peak. The diffraction peaks at 24.92°, 30.93°, 36.57°, 41.66°, 39.12°, and 54.62° can be attributed to the (110), (111), (002), (200), (102), and (202) planes of the monoclinic VO2 phase, with the lattice parameters of a = 4.5968 Å, b = 5.6844 Å, and c = 4.9133 Å, which have been reported in the literature [34].As can be seen from the figure, for CVH, no additional impurity diffraction peaks were clearly observed, and the characteristic VO2(D) peaks grew weaker. This shows that VO2(D) is successfully encapsulated by CQD and that neither low crystallinity CQD nor HG have an effect on VO2(D)'s crystal structure.

We performed Raman spectroscopic tests on CVH in the range of 0–2500 cm−1 to further evaluate its additional structural features. As can be seen in Fig. 3c, the VO2(D) sample's Raman spectrum exhibits two prominent peaks near 139 and 284 cm-1, which correspond to the V-O-V and V = O bonds, respectively. The peaks near 400 and 600 cm-1, on the other hand, are associated with the bridging modes of V3-O and V2-O, respectively. In the meantime, the band's 992 cm−1 location is where the stretching vibrations are found. These findings validate that VO2(D) was successfully fabricated [35]. Furthermore, the presence of graphene in the mixed body of CVH is further indicated by two distinct characteristic peaks near the D band (1350 cm−1) as well as the G band (1590 cm−1).

In order to understand the pore size of the synthesized materials as well as the number of pore sizes, we performed N2 physisorption tests. Mesoporous structures were present in CVH and VO2(D), which displayed characteristic Langmuir type IV adsorption coefficients at relative pressures (P/P0) of 0.037 to 1.0 and significant oscillation returns, as seen in Fig. 3d. Apparently, the BET SSA of CQD wrap VO2(D) anchored to the HG surface increased from 66.29 m2g−1 to 110.23 m2g−1 compared to the pristine VO2(D), and the higher specific surface area enhances the material's exposed active sites' interaction with the electrolyte, allowing for a greater number of active sites to be utilized for Zn2+ storage. HG offers a solid foundation for preserving the integrity of the self-supported structure in addition to serving as a platform for anchoring VO2(D) and an open, quick channel for ion/electron transport. The BJH pore size analysis of the CVH reveals that the maximum pore size distribution is concentrated at 2.14 and 14.25 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 3d. This porous structure slows down the volume expansion of the VO2(D) during cycling, which enhances the battery's multiplicity performance. It also provides an efficient channel for the quick diffusion and transport of electrolytes and electrons during charging and discharging.

The XPS analysis of the CVH material reveals the cohabitation of elements C, V, and O in the CVH hybridized body, as shown by the XPS analysis's measured spectra (Fig. 3a). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s are visible at 284.58, 286.48, and 288.98 eV, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 3b. These peaks correspond to CC, CO, and –COO [36,37]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c) exhibits three main peaks at binding energies of 530.3, 531.8, and 533.5 eV. The large peak at 530.3 eV indicates the presence of VOx, while the other two peaks at higher binding energies are attributed to the –OH bonds in HG and H2O water, respectively [38]. Fig. 3d illustrates the spectrum of V2P in CVH. The two fitted peaks at 516.08 and 523.78 eV for their binding energies are designated as V4+, while the other two peaks at 517.28 and 524.98 eV are associated with V5+. It is noteworthy that the presence of V5+ may be caused by the absorption of unreduced V2O5 on the surface of VO2 [39]. As an alternative, because of VO2′s sensitivity to air, V2O5 forms on its surface. The produced composites may have rapid electron transport and high electrical conductivity due to the coexistence of V4+ and V5+ [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of aqueous zinc-ion batteries electrochemical and kinetic
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the prepared sample's Zn2+ storage potential as a stand-alone ZIB cathode, an air-environment CR2016 button cell was constructed (details in the experimental section). Fig. 5 a, b shows the CV curves at CVH with a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 in the voltage range of 0.2–1.4 V (raw material voltage range of 0.2–1.5 V). The material's two pairs of cathode/anode peaks are situated at 0.78/1.01 and 0.56/0.91 V, as shown in Figure. a. These can be linked to the multistep Zn2+ embedding and de-embedding behaviors in VO2(D) [41]. Compared to the pristine VO2(D) material (Figure S3), the first cycle of the reaction process of CVH charging and discharging has a high overlap with the subsequent reaction cycle, suggesting that CVH has a highly reversible ability to carry out redox reactions [42]. The initial charge/discharge curves for the CVH and VO2(D) electrodes at a current density of 100 mA g−1 are displayed in Fig. 5c. Compared to pure VO2(D), which has a discharge capacity of 199 mAh/g, the CVH has a higher initial capacity of 443 mAh/g. The VO2(D) of CQD that is aided by the coating is responsible for the capacity gain. The advantage of CQD's special structure anchored on HG is that it produces more active sites and prevents the material's agglomerated VO2(D) dispersion. Furthermore, CVH demonstrated enhanced cycling performance, as depicted in Fig. 5d. With a poor capacity retention of 40.28 %, pure VO2(D) showed continuous capacity decay after 450 cycles at a current density of 500 mAh/g. This poor cycling performance was mainly caused by the collapse of the structure and vanadium dissolution during repeated Zn2+ embedding and de-embedding processes. (As shown in Fig. 5d.) The average discharge capacities of CVH at current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, were 443, 435, 416, 394, and 346mAh/g, respectively. indicating that CVH has a fast Zn storage capacity. In addition, when the current density is restored to 0.5 A/g, the specific capacity can be restored to 418 mAh/g due to sufficient activation, which shows that the multiplicative performance of CVH is greatly superior to that of VO2(D). Additionally, as seen in Fig. 5g, CVH exhibits excellent cycling performance. With a poor capacity retention of 40.28 %, pure VO2(D) shows continuous capacity decay after 450 cycles at a current density of 0.5A/g. The collapse of the structure and vanadium dissolution during repeated Zn2+ embedding/de-embedding processes were the primary causes of this subpar cycling performance. However, CVH has a greater capacity retention of 80.1 % at 1000 cycles, indicating that the stabilization of the CVH structure can effectively enhance cycling performance. This equates to a capacity decay rate of 0.0119 % per cycle. Meanwhile, we further examined the microstructure of the cathode using high-resolution TEM images after 1000 cycles (Figure S4). The lattice planes belonging to VO2, CQD, and HG can still be observed in this case. The results also show that CVH has good structural stability. It is worth noting that the capacity of the cell gradually decreases during the first 200 cycles due to the slow penetration of the electrolyte into the interlayer space of the VO2 layer [43,44]. The battery retains a high discharge specific capacity of 240 mAh/g after 350 cycles of a high-current cycling test at 3A/g. Additionally, the battery's charging and discharging efficiency is nearly 100 %, suggesting that there are fewer side reactions during the battery reaction.

When the CV curves at various scan rates (0.1–1.0 mV s−1) were tested in order to better understand the electrochemical reaction behavior of the CVH electrode, the results revealed that the CVH was superior to the pure VO2(D) in terms of reversible capacity, cyclic stability, and multiplicative capacity (Fig. 6 a). The broadening of the electrode polarization at high scan rates is responsible for the oxidation peaks' gradual movement to higher potentials and the reduction peaks' gradual movement to lower potentials as the scan rate increases, as seen in the figure. The kinetic equation of the reaction determines whether pseudocapacitive behavior occurs or not [45,46].(1)
(1)
i = a
v b

In this case, a and B are constants, v is the scan rate (V s−1), and i is the current (A). Generally, a diffusion-controlled process is indicated by B = 0.5, and a capacitor process is indicated by b = 1.0. Fig. 6(b) illustrates the relationship between log (i) and log (v). The slope of the redox peaks can be used to calculate the value of b. The four redox peaks' computed b-values were 0.81, 0.92, 0.90, and 0.83, which represent the capacity contribution from the pseudocapacitive behavior induced by the surface. The following formula can be used to determine the ratio of the Zn ion capacitive contribution (k1v) to the diffusive contribution (k2v) from the total capacity at a given scan rate [47,48]:(2)
(2)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
where the variables k1 and k2 are modifiable and change at various scan rates. This equation led to the conclusion that, at a scan rate of 0.5 mV/s, the capacitive contribution was approximately 76.5% (Fig. 6c). When the scan rate was raised from 0.1 to 1.0 mV/s, the capacitive contribution also increased, rising from 69.6% to 87.1% (Fig. 6d, S3). Compared to CVH, VO2(D) has a poorer pseudocapacitance contribution of 42.2% at a scan rate of 0.5 mv−1 and 58.5% at 1 mv−1 (Figure S6). This suggests that the capacitance contribution gradually increases with an increase in inscan rate, while the pseudocapacitance contribution predominates. It suggests that the surface reaction is accelerating the embedding and detachment of Zn2+ in the material more quickly than the internal diffusion reaction. As seen in Fig. 6f, the impedance of CVH and VO2(D) was assessed by EIS. The high-frequency region's semicircular radius reflects the charge transfer resistance's magnitude. The data clearly demonstrate that, under the same circumstances, the charge transfer impedance of CVH was measured to be 13.81 Ω, which is less than that of VO2(D). In addition, the Rcts of VO2(D) were 32.16 Ω, indicating that the charge transfer kinetics were greatly improved by encapsulating VO2(D) with a CQD coating while assisting its anchoring to the HG.

In addition, the diffusion coefficient was checked during the Zn2+ embedding and de-embedding process using the constant-current intermittent titration technique (GITT). Fig. 6g shows the GITT of CVH and its comparison material VO2(D), respectively, from which it can be seen that the calculated values of the diffusion coefficient of the CVH electrode are higher than those of the VO2(D) electrode during both the discharge and charging processes, indicating that the structure of CQD-coating-modified VO2 deposited on the surface of HG has a fast Zn2+ migration property. The fast Zn2+ migration property of the CQD-coated modified VO2 structure deposited on the HG surface is demonstrated. This property can be attributed to both the surface modification of the quantum dots and the HG composite, which can offer more active sites and electron-transporting channels, thereby promoting the electrochemical reaction and raising its diffusion coefficient.

In summary, CQD-coated VO2(D) and welded to HG (CVH) were prepared by a simple solvothermal as well as ultrasonic self-assembly method for advanced aqueous zinc-ion battery cathode materials, and the structural and interfacial stability was achieved by the construction of a stable interface between VO2(D) and HG, which could facilitate highly reversible conversion and pseudocapacitive reactions. Meanwhile, CQD has great flexibility in adsorbing on the surface of VO2(D), showing great advantages the not only reduce the buildup and dissolution of the material but also improve the storage performance of zinc. Meanwhile, the holes in the HG provide longitudinal channels for ion/electron diffusion, which greatly accelerates the ion/electron diffusion efficiency. The obtained CVH achieved a capacity of 443 mAh/g at 0.1 A/g, and still had a capacity retention of 80.1 % after 1000 cycles at a current density of 0.5 A/g. At the same time, it still has a better performance of 240.1 mAh/g after 350 cycles at 3A/g. Therefore, this study provides a new idea for designing VO2(D) as the cathode material of ZIB.
Luchen Wu: . Xinyu Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Guilin Lu: Methodology, Supervision. Yiwen Wang: Data curation. Zhen Zhang: Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region of China(2018GXNSFAA281191), Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices (20KF-20).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2023.118006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
